channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - techraptor.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for techraptor.net

item.title: PlayStation Playerbase Wants to Scan You Into PlayStation Games
item.content:encoded:
Sony has announced a new PlayStation initiative it's calling Playerbase, and it's asking players to let the company scan them into games as in-game characters and more.

Playerbase was announced in a PlayStation Blog post earlier today. Sony describes it as a unique opportunity to immortalize yourself in some of PlayStation's games by "having [your] likeness scanned and appear[ing] within a PlayStation game".

Initially, Playerbase will introduce a lucky PlayStation fan to the world of racing sim Gran Turismo 7, but Sony says it's hoping to expand the initiative out so that it encapsulates other first-party PlayStation Studios games and worlds as well. I suppose whether that happens will depend on the success of this first outing.

If you apply to sign up with Playerbase, you'll be asked "a few questions about your experiences on PlayStation". Of course, you'll need a PlayStation account if you want to participate.

Once all of the applications are in, Sony says it'll go through them and invite those who have been selected to video interviews where they'll need to talk about "their connection to PlayStation". Only one person will be selected in the end, so you'd better make sure your application is sharp if you want to succeed.

If you are selected, you'll have a "special limited-time appearance as an in-game character portrait", and you'll also get the chance to design a Fantasy Logo and a livery, both of which will remain available in Gran Turismo 7 indefinitely.

News of this latest intiative comes after the announcement of a major PlayStation price hike back in March, as well as reports that Sony's iconic PlayStation Network branding will be dropped by September (although we don't know what it will be replaced with yet).

If you want to be in with a chance of being scanned into Gran Turismo 7 with Playerbase, you can visit the official PlayStation website to sign up. That link also contains the official rules for the initiative, so make sure to have a read of those before you put your application in.

Joe has been writing for TechRaptor for several years, and in those years has learned a lot about the gaming industry and its foibles. He’s originally an… More about Joseph



https://techraptor.net/gaming/news/playstation-playerbase-scanning-games
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: People of Note Review - Battle Tested For The Stage
item.content:encoded:
People of Note opens talking about the song contest at the center of the game’s narrative: Noteworthy, very much this game’s version of the popular Eurovision song contest. Don’t give the people what they want, the game’s protagonist, Cadence, asserts. Give them something they have no idea they’re looking for.

I didn’t have People of Note on my radar until I played the preview build early last year, and it instantly became one of my most anticipated games of the year. This new RPG brilliantly remixes and combines genre staples to make something unique and fulfilling, even if there are some places it’s still finding its own voice.

A Swell Battle

People of Note is a turn-based RPG, heavily inspired by PlayStation era games like Final Fantasy VII and VIII specifically. The fixed camera environments of that era are on display, letting you take in the gorgeous and varied world of Note in its full beauty.

It’s not fully one-to-one to this style, though. Random encounters are nowhere to be found; instead, fixed battles are in each dungeon, while you can also “drop a beat” to find enemies on demand to gather resources or grind. It’s a great middle ground on the battles of old.

Once you’re in combat, you get to experience People of Note’s inventive battle system, revolving around Stanzas and Measures. Each turn is a Stanza. The time signature denotes how many actions each team gets. For example, if a Stanza’s time signature says 3/4, the good guys get 3 actions, and the bad guys get 4. You use all your actions, then the enemies use all of theirs.

Perhaps the greatest achievement of the development team is how fun this system is. Measures can be modified by both you and your enemies. For example, an enemy might make your abilities cost more in the second measure of each stanza, or you can double your damage in the first measure of every turn. There are a lot of ways fights can play out, and the game rewards you for taking your time to create the perfect stanza.

Battles are difficult, but not punishing. If you lose, you’re placed right outside the fight you lost, losing 1 of an area-specific currency as a penalty. It’s very easy to regain this currency, and it feels like a fair penalty. 

This lets you play around with how you approach each battle immediately after seeing it. Should you try different abilities? Do things in a different order? This kind of experimentation is encouraged, and in many cases feels even necessary.

A unique type of battle, Puzzle Battles, found in various cities in Note, shows you the capabilities of this system. They give your party set abilities, and a certain task: for instance, “deal 200 damage with one attack.” Then, you have to puzzle out how to boost stats, manipulate measures, and do it in the correct order to make it happen. They’re really well designed and teach you about the capabilities in your hand. I’ve never played anything quite like it.

Everything in the world looks great, too. Many dialogue exchanges show hand-drawn portraits of the characters, which all express the band members’ personalities in really fun ways. Environments are a lot of fun to traverse, and typically have some sort of musical theming like the “Wood Winds” from an early area, Lilting Green.

Of course, it wouldn’t be a musical RPG without musical numbers. While none of the environmental or battle music stands out as great, some of the musical numbers in the latter half of the game are really fun. The boy band Smolder has a great song with “Sorry Girl,” which I thought was a point where the game’s humor really worked in its favor. Also, Joe Zieja (perhaps best known for playing Claude in Fire Emblem: Three Houses) has some pipes, man.

The Dialogue Doldrums

People of Note starts as a journey Cadence takes to recruit a band to win the Noteworthy song contest, but quickly blossoms into a story with much larger stakes as Cadence meets more bandmates with their own motivations. It’s a cohesive enough narrative to push you from dungeon to dungeon, but the dialogue it’s told with can often be a chore to get through.

Every dialogue exchange is filled to the brim with references. References to songs, to memes, and at a few points, the fact that it’s a video game.  It’s one thing if there were a few spread around the world, but they frequently permeate into even main story exchanges.

In one section, two party members hatch a plan to pretend to be mechanics to sneak into an area. “Stealth Mechanics?” one of the characters quips. “That would never work.” It starts off a little charming, but quickly becomes grating.

Luckily, the characters are likeable, and the party has a fun dynamic. Some midgame drama felt a little rushed, but the plot is at least serviceable. The aforementioned references take away from some late emotional beats, but it buttons up enough to deliver a satisfying finale.

People of Note | Final Thoughts

Iridium Studios is clearly filled with great ideas: the turn-based combat system is a blast, the environment and puzzle design are sharp, and their characters look great. Most importantly, all of these systems are things all their own. 

People of Note is a great RPG. Where I feel it gets a little bit dragged down is everywhere it stays close to the familiar, with its rapid-fire references and fourth-wall breaking. With a narrative that’s unapologetically its own and dialogue that serves a stronger, more cohesive narrative, I think the team’s next game could go from “a great RPG” to “one of the great RPGs.”

People of Note was reviewed on PlayStation 5 after 20 hours of gameplay. A copy for review was provided by the publisher.

Parker is a freelance writer based in Iowa, and the co-founder and editor of gaming satire site Palette Swap. Before Palette Swap, he was an editor at Hard… More about Parker



https://techraptor.net/gaming/reviews/people-of-note-review
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Frostpunk 2 Breach of Trust DLC Heats Up in June
item.content:encoded:
11 bit studios has revealed a brand new Frostpunk 2 DLC pack, and this one will see you dealing with a city built near "a volcano destabilised by the reckless use of geothermal energy".

The city-builder received its first major DLC in the form of Fractured Utopias back in December, but now the second big expansion, Breach of Trust, is coming on June 23rd. It's set in the city of New Edinburgh, where "unrest rises and volcanic ash fills the sky".

The DLC is named for a new mechanic, the Vote of Trust, which will see citizens of New Edinburgh periodically voting on whether they think you're still fit to run the city or not. You'll have to unravel the story of New Edinburgh and its colony, Aurora, while also keeping the city's inhabitants happy with your methods.

New features promised by Breach of Trust include five new Communities and Factions, as well as new environmental hazards (which are naturally themed around the aforementioned volcano), new narrative events, and a brand new Independent Colony system to help you decide the fate of the Aurora colony.

Along with the name and release date of the new DLC, 11 bit has also revealed the codename for the next DLC: "Surge". Could this imply some kind of focus around flooding? The studio says it'll release Surge later this year, so expect more info about that in the coming months.

While you wait, if you'd like to get the chance to playtest Breach of Trust, you can sign up for the upcoming closed beta right here. 11 bit says it'll announce the start date for the beta "in the near future".

Frostpunk 2 is available right now on PC, PlayStation 5, and Xbox Series X|S, and you can check out our review here. Breach of Trust launches on June 23rd.

Joe has been writing for TechRaptor for several years, and in those years has learned a lot about the gaming industry and its foibles. He’s originally an… More about Joseph



https://techraptor.net/gaming/news/frostpunk-2-breach-of-trust-dlc-announcement
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Mario Maker 2 Level Deletion Spree Possibly Traced to Infamous Cheater
item.content:encoded:
Hundreds upon hundreds of Super Mario Maker 2 levels have been deleted by Nintendo in the last few days, and the source of said deletions may have been traced to a single salty cheater.

As spotted by ResetEra user Maedhros, several Mario Maker 2 creators who are part of the Team Shell user community have been reporting that their levels have been taken down by Nintendo, and while the only reason that's been given is a rather vague reference to "advertising", many of these levels contain hashtags as markers of those who created them.

Given that Nintendo has a history of overzealously pursuing complaints it believes it has against its community and businesses around it, there is a pretty big chance that these deletions are being actively pursued by Nintendo itself. However, there's another possibility.

As reported by Kotaku, a user of a particular Discord server (although not Team Shell's) with the username LMT has taken credit for being behind the reports that led to the levels in question being deleted. It's worth noting that some of the levels have been up for several years and haven't drawn Nintendo's ire, so this sudden movement seems suspiciously-timed.

According to Kotaku, LMT's profile links to a YouTube channel that points to a user called MT94, who was, at one point, a well-respected member of the Super Mario Maker 2 community. However, when it was discovered that MT94 had cheated to reach a high ranking on the game's leaderboards, they fell from grace.

In a (self-censored) tirade against their Discord server's users, LMT says they will "keep going" with the deletion reports, laughing at those whose levels have been removed from Super Mario Maker 2 and pledging to "destroy everything" using the "5 Switches" they currently have set to report stages.

Using multiple Switches to achieve their goals is consistent with MT94's previous behavior; apparently, that's how they achieved a high rank in the first place, with multiple consoles being used to stage fake competitive battles between "users" (which were really just different accounts held by MT94).

It's possible, of course, that MT94 is just trying to claim ownership of a phenomenon that suits their particular grudge, but the timing of the posts, together with the vehemence with which the user is posting, seems odd if that's the case.

Unfortunately, it looks like accounts associated with deleted levels are also being banned, likely thanks to some kind of automated system that's in place on Nintendo's part. If you've got Super Mario Maker 2 levels uploaded as part of the Team Shell Discord community, you may want to re-evaluate your uploads.

Joe has been writing for TechRaptor for several years, and in those years has learned a lot about the gaming industry and its foibles. He’s originally an… More about Joseph



https://techraptor.net/gaming/news/mario-maker-2-level-deletion-spree
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
